medications.indication_rationale: First-line for plantar warts, applied to the pared/filed lesion daily; source prescribed a topical salicylic acid preparation daily.
medications.dose_route_frequency: apply to the lesion daily after filing; continue for weeks (as per source note)

medications.indication_rationale: Paring the callus and a 'donut' pad redistribute weight and immediately reduce pain; source did paring + padding (in-person).

medications.drug_name: self-cutting/'bathroom surgery' / aggressive acid on surrounding normal skin or in a diabetic foot
medications.indication_rationale: Self-excision or applying strong keratolytics to surrounding skin (or any acid in a diabetic/neuropathic/ischaemic foot) risks ulceration/infection — avoid.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Soak and file the dead white skin before each salicylic-acid application; keep the foot dry; wear thongs/flip-flops in communal showers; don't share shoes/socks/towels.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Filing lets the treatment reach the wart; footwear/hygiene reduces spread and reinfection.

patient_education_points.point: This is a plantar wart — a harmless viral lump on the sole (the black dots are its tiny blood vessels). Paring and padding relieve the pain immediately; the wart itself takes weeks to months of daily acid treatment to clear.
File the hard skin before applying the acid each day, wear thongs in communal showers, and don't share shoes/towels. A podiatrist can offer paring or freezing (cryotherapy).

See a doctor if the area becomes red, hot, swollen or leaks pus, or you get a red streak up the foot or a fever (infection), or if the lesion looks atypical, changes colour, bleeds on its own or doesn't clear — it should be checked (and biopsied if needed).

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Pain relief/offloading; cryotherapy/paring; biopsy if atypical/non-resolving.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Secondary infection / diabetic foot / atypical lesion — GP/podiatry.

must_include_items: Distinguish plantar wart from a corn (skin lines/black dots/lateral pinch)
Topical salicylic acid + filing + offloading padding; podiatry for paring/cryotherapy
Screen/safety-net secondary infection, diabetic foot, and atypical (malignant) features
errors_of_commission: Advising self-excision / strong acid on a diabetic or neuropathic foot
Missing plantar melanoma in an atypical lesion
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: Pass requires correct wart-vs-corn reasoning, salicylic-acid/offloading management with podiatry, and a malignancy/infection/diabetic-foot safety-net.
